**Reformed Christianity**, also called **Calvinism**,[[a]](#cite_note-2) is a major branch of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") that began during the 16th-century [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). In the modern day, it is largely represented by the [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), and [Congregational](/wiki/Congregationalism "Congregationalism") traditions, as well as parts of the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") (known as "Episcopal" in some regions), [Baptist](/wiki/Reformed_Baptists "Reformed Baptists") and [Waldensian](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") traditions, in addition to a minority of persons belonging to the [Methodist](/wiki/Calvinistic_Methodist "Calvinistic Methodist") faith (who are known as Calvinistic Methodists).

Reformed theology emphasizes the [authority of the Bible](/wiki/Biblical_authority "Biblical authority") and the [sovereignty of God](/wiki/Sovereignty_of_God_in_Christianity "Sovereignty of God in Christianity"), as well as [covenant theology](/wiki/Covenant_theology "Covenant theology"), a framework for understanding the Bible based on God's covenants with people. Reformed churches emphasize simplicity in worship. Several forms of [ecclesiastical polity](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity") are exercised by Reformed churches, including [presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity"), [congregational](/wiki/Congregational_polity "Congregational polity"), and some [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity"). Articulated by theologian [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), the Reformed faith holds to a [spiritual (pneumatic) presence](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology") of Christ in the [Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist").

Emerging in the 16th century, the Reformed tradition developed over several generations, especially in areas of [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"). In the 17th century, [Jacobus Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius") and the [Remonstrants](/wiki/Remonstrants "Remonstrants") were expelled from the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") over disputes regarding [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") and [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity"), and from that time [Arminians](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") are usually considered to be a distinct tradition from the Reformed. This dispute produced the [Canons of Dort](/wiki/Canons_of_Dort "Canons of Dort"), the basis for the "doctrines of grace" also known as the ["five points" of Calvinism](/wiki/Five_Points_of_Calvinism "Five Points of Calvinism").

Calvinism influenced social, economic, and political life by promoting [hard work](/wiki/Diligence "Diligence"), [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade"), and [wealth accumulation](/wiki/Capital_accumulation "Capital accumulation") within ethical limits, laying the groundwork for modern capitalism, especially in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). Its emphasis on elected church elders, the [priesthood of all believers](/wiki/Priesthood_of_all_believers "Priesthood of all believers"), and [mixed government](/wiki/Mixed_government "Mixed government") inspired early democratic practices, [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"), and protections for [religious minorities](/wiki/Minority_religion "Minority religion"), shaping colonies in North America and [liberal political thought](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") in England. Calvinist-inspired reforms also advanced social causes like [abolition](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism"), [women’s suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage"), education, and humanitarian efforts worldwide.

## Definition and terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition and terminology")]

The term Reformed Christianity is derived from the denomination's self designation of "Reformed Church", beginning in Switzerland and Germany, shortly thereafter followed by the Dutch Republic. *Calvinism* is the name derived from its most famous leader, [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") (born Jehan Cauvin), an influential Reformation-era theologian from [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"), Switzerland. The term was first used by opposing [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") in the 1550s. Calvin did not approve of the use of this term,[[2]](#cite_note-Cottret2003-3) and religious scholars have argued its use is misleading, inaccurate, unhelpful,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen20103–4-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuller2004130-1) and "inherently distortive."[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The definitions and boundaries of the terms *Reformed Christianity* and *Calvinism* are contested by scholars. As a historical movement, Reformed Christianity began during the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") with [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli") in [Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich"), Switzerland. Following the failure of the [Marburg Colloquy](/wiki/Marburg_Colloquy "Marburg Colloquy") between Zwingli's followers and those of [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") in 1529 to mediate disputes regarding the [real presence of Christ in the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist"), Zwingli's followers were defined by their opposition to Lutherans (while Lutherans affirmed a corporeal presence of Christ in the Eucharist through a [sacramental union](/wiki/Sacramental_union "Sacramental union"), the Reformed came to hold a real spiritual presence of Christ in the Eucharist as propounded by Calvin and Bullinger).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch2005174-9) They also opposed [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism") radicals[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch2005184-10) thus remaining within the [Magisterial Reformation](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation").[[10]](#cite_note-Voorst2014-11)[[11]](#cite_note-mcgrath159-12) During the 17th-century [Arminian Controversy](/wiki/History_of_the_Calvinist%E2%80%93Arminian_debate "History of the Calvinist–Arminian debate"), followers of [Jacobus Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius") were forcibly removed from the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") for their views regarding [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") and [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity"), and thenceforth [Arminians](/wiki/Arminians "Arminians") would be considered outside the pale of Reformed orthodoxy,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch2005378-13) though some use the term *Reformed* to include Arminians while using the term *Calvinist* to exclude Arminians.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Reformed Christianity has historically [included Anglicanism](/wiki/Reformed_theology_in_Anglicanism "Reformed theology in Anglicanism"), the branch of Christianity originating in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[14]](#cite_note-González1987-15) The Anglican confessions are considered Reformed Protestant[[15]](#cite_note-16) and leaders of the [Protestant Reformation in England](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), such as the guiding [Reformer](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers") who shaped Anglican theology [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), were influenced by and counted among Reformed (Calvinist) theologians.[[14]](#cite_note-González1987-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Elwell2001-17)[[17]](#cite_note-Samuel2020-18) As with [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), the Church of England retained elements of [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") such as [bishops](/wiki/Bishops_in_Calvinism "Bishops in Calvinism") and [vestments](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments"), thus sometimes being called "but halfly Reformed" or a middle way between Lutheranism and Reformed Christianity, being closer liturgically to the former and theologically aligned with the latter.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-AEH2003-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) Beginning in the 17th century, Anglicanism broadened to the extent that Reformed theology is no longer the sole dominant theology of Anglicanism.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Some scholars argue that the [Particular Baptist](/wiki/Particular_Baptist "Particular Baptist") (Reformed Baptist) strand of the [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") tradition, who hold many of the same beliefs as Reformed Christians but not [infant baptism](/wiki/Infant_baptism "Infant baptism"), as expressed in the [Second London Confession of Faith of 1689](/wiki/1689_Baptist_Confession_of_Faith "1689 Baptist Confession of Faith"), should be considered part of Reformed Christianity, though this might not have been the view of early Reformed theologians.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Others disagree, asserting that any type of [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") should be considered separate from the Reformed branch of Christianity.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Reformed Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Reformed_Christianity "History of Reformed Christianity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Geneva_Cathedral.jpg/330px-Geneva_Cathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geneva_Cathedral.jpg)

Calvin preached at [St. Pierre Cathedral](/wiki/St._Pierre_Cathedral "St. Pierre Cathedral") in [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva").

The first wave of Reformed theologians included Zwingli, [Martin Bucer](/wiki/Martin_Bucer "Martin Bucer"), [Wolfgang Capito](/wiki/Wolfgang_Capito "Wolfgang Capito"), [John Oecolampadius](/wiki/John_Oecolampadius "John Oecolampadius"), and [Guillaume Farel](/wiki/Guillaume_Farel "Guillaume Farel"). While from diverse academic backgrounds, their work already contained key themes within Reformed theology, especially the priority of [scripture](/wiki/Bible "Bible") as a source of authority. Scripture was also viewed as a unified whole, which led to a [covenantal theology](/wiki/Covenant_theology "Covenant theology") of the [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments "Sacraments") of [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") and the [Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") as visible signs of the covenant of grace. Another shared perspective was their denial of the real presence of Christ in the Eucharist. Each understood salvation to be by [grace alone](/wiki/Sola_gratia "Sola gratia") and affirmed a doctrine of [unconditional election](/wiki/Unconditional_election "Unconditional election"), the teaching that some people are chosen by God to be [saved](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity"). Luther and his successor [Philipp Melanchthon](/wiki/Philipp_Melanchthon "Philipp Melanchthon") were significant influences on these theologians and, to a larger extent, those who followed. The doctrine of justification by faith alone, also known as *[sola fide](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide")*,[[24]](#cite_note-25) was a direct inheritance from Luther.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuller2004131–132-26)

The second generation featured [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), [Heinrich Bullinger](/wiki/Heinrich_Bullinger "Heinrich Bullinger"), [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), [Wolfgang Musculus](/wiki/Wolfgang_Musculus "Wolfgang Musculus"), [Peter Martyr Vermigli](/wiki/Peter_Martyr_Vermigli "Peter Martyr Vermigli"), [Andreas Hyperius](/wiki/Andreas_Hyperius "Andreas Hyperius") and [John à Lasco](/wiki/Jan_%C5%81aski "Jan Łaski"). Written between 1536 and 1539, Calvin's *[Institutes of the Christian Religion](/wiki/Institutes_of_the_Christian_Religion "Institutes of the Christian Religion")* was one of the most influential works of the era.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuller2004132-27) Toward the middle of the 16th century, these beliefs were formed into one consistent [creed](/wiki/Creed "Creed") which would shape the future definition of the Reformed faith. The 1549 *[Consensus Tigurinus](/wiki/Consensus_Tigurinus "Consensus Tigurinus")* unified Zwingli and Bullinger's [memorialist](/wiki/Memorialism "Memorialism") theology of the Eucharist, which taught that it was simply a reminder of Christ's death, with Calvin's view of it as a [means of grace](/wiki/Means_of_grace "Means of grace") with Christ actually present, though spiritually rather than bodily as in Catholic doctrine. The document demonstrates the diversity as well as unity in early Reformed theology, giving it a stability that enabled it to spread rapidly throughout Europe. This stands in marked contrast to the bitter controversy experienced by Lutherans prior to the 1579 [Formula of Concord](/wiki/Formula_of_Concord "Formula of Concord").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMuller2004135-28)

Through Calvin's missionary work in France, his program of reform eventually reached the French-speaking provinces of the Netherlands. Calvinism was adopted in the [Electorate of the Palatinate](/wiki/Electorate_of_the_Palatinate "Electorate of the Palatinate") under [Frederick III](/wiki/Frederick_III,_Elector_Palatine "Frederick III, Elector Palatine"), which led to the formulation of the [Heidelberg Catechism](/wiki/Heidelberg_Catechism "Heidelberg Catechism") in 1563. This and the [Belgic Confession](/wiki/Belgic_Confession "Belgic Confession") were adopted as confessional standards in the [first synod](/wiki/Synod_of_Emden "Synod of Emden") of the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") in 1571.

In 1573, [William the Silent](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") joined the Calvinist Church. Calvinism was declared the official religion of the [Kingdom of Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") by the queen regnant [Jeanne d'Albret](/wiki/Jeanne_d%27Albret "Jeanne d'Albret") after her conversion in 1560. Leading divines, either Calvinist or those sympathetic to Calvinism, settled in England, including Bucer, Martyr, and [John Łaski](/wiki/Jan_%C5%81aski "Jan Łaski"), as did [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox") in Scotland. During the [First English Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War"), English and Scots [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterians "Presbyterians") produced the [Westminster Confession](/wiki/Westminster_Confession "Westminster Confession"), which became the confessional standard for Presbyterians in the English-speaking world. Having established itself in Europe, the movement continued to spread to areas including North America, South Africa and Korea.[[28]](#cite_note-29) While Calvin did not live to see the foundation of his work grow into an international movement, his death allowed his ideas to spread far beyond their city of origin and their borders and to establish their own distinct character.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPettegree2004222-30)

### Spread

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Spread")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Interior_of_the_Oude_kerk_in_Amsterdam_%28south_nave%29%2C_by_Emanuel_de_Witte.jpg/330px-Interior_of_the_Oude_kerk_in_Amsterdam_%28south_nave%29%2C_by_Emanuel_de_Witte.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_of_the_Oude_kerk_in_Amsterdam_(south_nave),_by_Emanuel_de_Witte.jpg)

Early Calvinism was known for simple, unadorned churches as depicted in this 1661 portrait of the interior of the [Oude Kerk, Amsterdam](/wiki/Oude_Kerk,_Amsterdam "Oude Kerk, Amsterdam").

Although much of Calvin's work was in [Geneva](/wiki/Calvin%27s_Geneva "Calvin's Geneva"), his publications spread his ideas of a correctly Reformed church to many parts of Europe. In Switzerland, some cantons are still Reformed, and some are Catholic. Calvinism became the dominant doctrine within the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") (Presbyterian Church), the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and parts of Germany, especially those adjacent to the Netherlands in the [Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_(region) "Palatinate (region)"), [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), and [Lippe](/wiki/Lippe_(district) "Lippe (district)"), spread by [Caspar Olevian](/wiki/Caspar_Olevian "Caspar Olevian") and [Zacharias Ursinus](/wiki/Zacharias_Ursinus "Zacharias Ursinus") among others. Protected by the local nobility, Calvinism became a significant religion in eastern Hungary and Hungarian-speaking areas of [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"). As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit), there are about 3.5 million Hungarian Reformed people worldwide.[[30]](#cite_note-The_Reformed_Church-31)

Calvinism was also initially spreading in Flanders, Wallonia, France, Lithuania, and Poland before being mostly erased during the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation"). One of the most important Polish reformed theologists was Łaski, who was also involved into organising churches in [East Frisia](/wiki/East_Frisia "East Frisia") and [Stranger's Church](/wiki/Stranger_churches "Stranger churches") in London.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Later, a faction called the [Polish Brethren](/wiki/Polish_Brethren "Polish Brethren") broke away from Calvinism on January 22, 1556, when [Piotr of Goniądz](/wiki/Piotr_of_Goni%C4%85dz "Piotr of Goniądz"), a Polish student, spoke out against the doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") during the general synod of the Reformed churches of Poland held in the village of [Secemin](/wiki/Secemin "Secemin").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Calvinism gained some popularity in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), especially Sweden, but was rejected in favor of Lutheranism after the [Synod of Uppsala](/wiki/Synod_of_Uppsala "Synod of Uppsala") in 1593.[[33]](#cite_note-Vlib.iue.it-34)

Many 17th century European settlers in the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America") were Calvinists, who emigrated because of arguments over church structure, including the [Pilgrim Fathers](/wiki/Pilgrims_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrims (Plymouth Colony)"). Others were forced into exile, including the French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots"). Dutch and French Calvinist settlers were also among the first European colonizers of South Africa, beginning in the 17th century, who became known as [Boers](/wiki/Boers "Boers") or [Afrikaners](/wiki/Afrikaner_Calvinism "Afrikaner Calvinism").

Sierra Leone was largely colonized by Calvinist settlers from Nova Scotia, many of whom were [Black Loyalists](/wiki/Black_Loyalist "Black Loyalist") who fought for the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") during the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). [John Marrant](/wiki/John_Marrant "John Marrant") had organized a congregation there under the auspices of the [Huntingdon Connection](/wiki/Countess_of_Huntingdon%27s_Connexion "Countess of Huntingdon's Connexion"). Some of the largest Calvinist communions were started by 19th- and 20th-century [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary"). Especially large are those in Indonesia, Korea and Nigeria. In South Korea there are 20,000 [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") congregations with about 9–10 million church members, scattered in more than 100 Presbyterian denominations. In South Korea, Presbyterianism is the largest Christian denomination.[[34]](#cite_note-Chris_Meehan-35)

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Demography")]

A 2011 report of the [Pew Forum](/wiki/Pew_Forum "Pew Forum") on Religious and Public Life estimates that members of Presbyterian or Reformed churches make up 7% of the estimated 801 million Protestants globally, or approximately 56 million people.[[35]](#cite_note-Pew_Research_Center's_Forum_on_Religion_and_Public_Life_21,_70-36)

Though the broadly defined Reformed faith is much larger, as it constitutes Congregationalist (0.5%), most of the United and uniting churches (unions of different denominations) (7.2%) and most likely some of the other Protestant denominations (38.2%). All three are distinct categories from Presbyterian or Reformed (7%) in this report. The Reformed family of churches is one of the largest Christian denominations, representing 75 million believers worldwide.[[36]](#cite_note-adherents.com-37)

According to *Global Christianity: A Guide to the World's Largest Religion from Afghanistan to Zimbabwe*, in 2020, Presbyterian and Reformed Christians numbered around 65,446,000 people, or 0.8% of the world's population. Congregationalists were listed at 4,986,000, with 0.1% of the world's population. Therefore, the three branches of Reformed Christianity totaled 70,432,000 people, or 0.9% of the global population.[[37]](#cite_note-Statisticglobal2020-38)

The survey also listed 77,792,000 members (1% of the world's population) in [United Churches](/wiki/United_Churches "United Churches"), the majority of which are formed by the merger of churches of the Reformed Tradition with churches of other branches of Protestantism.[[37]](#cite_note-Statisticglobal2020-38)

## World Communions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: World Communions")]

The [World Communion of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Communion_of_Reformed_Churches "World Communion of Reformed Churches") (WCRC), which includes some [United Churches](/wiki/United_Churches "United Churches"), has 80 million believers.[[38]](#cite_note-WCRC_History-39) WCRC is the fourth largest Christian communion in the world, after the Roman Catholic Church, the Eastern Orthodox Churches, and the Anglican Communion.[[36]](#cite_note-adherents.com-37) Many conservative Reformed churches which are strongly Calvinistic formed the [World Reformed Fellowship](/wiki/World_Reformed_Fellowship "World Reformed Fellowship") which has about 70 member denominations. Most are not part of the WCRC because of its ecumenical attire. The [International Conference of Reformed Churches](/wiki/International_Conference_of_Reformed_Churches "International Conference of Reformed Churches") is another conservative association.

## Theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Theology")]

### Revelation and scripture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Revelation and scripture")]

See also: [General revelation](/wiki/General_revelation "General revelation"), [Biblical inspiration](/wiki/Biblical_inspiration "Biblical inspiration"), and [Sola scriptura](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America_no_background.png/330px-Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America_no_background.png)](/wiki/File:Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America_no_background.png)

The seal of the [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America"), an early American Presbyterian church founded in 1789

Reformed theologians believe that God communicates knowledge of himself to people through the Word of God. People are not able to know anything about God except through this self-revelation. (With the exception of general revelation of God; "His invisible attributes, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly seen, being understood through what has been made, so that they are without excuse" (Romans 1:20).) Speculation about anything which God has not revealed through his Word is not warranted. The knowledge people have of God is different from that which they have of anything else because God is [infinite](/wiki/Infinity_(philosophy) "Infinity (philosophy)"), and finite people are incapable of comprehending an infinite being. While the knowledge revealed by God to people is never incorrect, it is also never comprehensive.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201018–20-40)

According to Reformed theologians, God's self-revelation is always through his son [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") Christ, because Christ is the only mediator between God and people. Revelation of God through Christ comes through two basic channels. The first is [creation](/wiki/Genesis_creation_narrative "Genesis creation narrative") and [providence](/wiki/Divine_providence "Divine providence"), which is God's creating and continuing to work in the world. This action of God gives everyone knowledge about God, but this knowledge is only sufficient to make people culpable for their sin; it does not include knowledge of the gospel. The second channel through which God reveals himself is [redemption](/wiki/Redemption_(theology) "Redemption (theology)"), which is the gospel of [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity") from condemnation which is punishment for sin.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201022–23-41)

In Reformed theology, the Word of God takes several forms. Jesus Christ is the Word Incarnate. The prophecies about him said to be found in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") and the ministry of the [apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament") who saw him and communicated his message are also the Word of God. Further, the [preaching](/wiki/Sermon "Sermon") of ministers about God is the very Word of God because God is considered to be speaking through them. God also speaks through human writers in the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), which is composed of texts set apart by God for self-revelation.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201024–25-42) Reformed theologians emphasize the Bible as a uniquely important means by which God communicates with people. People gain knowledge of God from the Bible which cannot be gained in any other way.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200112-43)

Reformed theologians affirm that the Bible is true, but differences emerge among them over the meaning and extent of its truthfulness.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201028-44) Conservative followers of the [Princeton theologians](/wiki/Princeton_theologians "Princeton theologians") take the view that the Bible is true and [inerrant](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy"), or incapable of error or falsehood, in every place.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201031-45) This view is similar to that of [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") orthodoxy as well as modern [Evangelicalism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarleyHodgson199477-46) Another view, influenced by the teaching of [Karl Barth](/wiki/Karl_Barth "Karl Barth") and [neo-orthodoxy](/wiki/Neo-orthodoxy "Neo-orthodoxy"), is found in the [Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(U.S.A.) "Presbyterian Church (U.S.A.)")'s [Confession of 1967](/wiki/Confession_of_1967 "Confession of 1967"). Those who take this view believe the Bible to be the primary source of our knowledge of God, but also that some parts of the Bible may be false, not witnesses to Christ, and not normative for the church.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201031-45) In this view, Christ is the revelation of God, and the scriptures witness to this revelation rather than being the revelation itself.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200120-47)

### Covenant theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Covenant theology")]

Main article: [Covenant theology](/wiki/Covenant_theology "Covenant theology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Jacob_Jordaens_-_The_Fall_of_Man_-_WGA12014.jpg/330px-Jacob_Jordaens_-_The_Fall_of_Man_-_WGA12014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacob_Jordaens_-_The_Fall_of_Man_-_WGA12014.jpg)

*Fall of Man* by [Jacob Jordaens](/wiki/Jacob_Jordaens "Jacob Jordaens")

Reformed theologians use the concept of covenant to describe the way God enters into fellowship with people in history.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201034–35-48) The concept of covenant is so prominent in Reformed theology that Reformed theology as a whole is sometimes called "covenant theology".[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001230_n._28-49) However, sixteenth- and seventeenth-century theologians developed a particular theological system called "[covenant theology](/wiki/Covenant_theology "Covenant theology")" or "federal theology" which many conservative Reformed churches continue to affirm.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201034–35-48) This framework orders God's life with people primarily in two covenants: the covenant of works and the covenant of grace.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201044-50)

The covenant of works is made with [Adam and Eve](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve "Adam and Eve") in the [Garden of Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden"). The terms of the covenant are that God provides a blessed life in the garden on condition that Adam and Eve obey God's law perfectly. Because Adam and Eve broke the covenant by eating the [forbidden fruit](/wiki/Forbidden_fruit "Forbidden fruit"), they became subject to death and were banished from the garden. This sin was passed down to all mankind because all people are said to be in Adam as a covenantal or "federal" head. Federal theologians usually imply that Adam and Eve would have gained immortality had they obeyed perfectly.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201041–42-51)

A second covenant, called the covenant of grace, is said to have been made immediately following Adam and Eve's sin. In it, God graciously offers salvation from death on condition of faith in God. This covenant is administered in different ways throughout the Old and New Testaments, but retains the substance of being free of a requirement of perfect obedience.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201043-52)

Through the influence of Karl Barth, many contemporary Reformed theologians have discarded the covenant of works, along with other concepts of federal theology. Barth saw the covenant of works as disconnected from Christ and the gospel, and rejected the idea that God works with people in this way. Instead, Barth argued that God always interacts with people under the covenant of grace, and that the covenant of grace is free of all conditions whatsoever. Barth's theology and that which follows him has been called "mono covenantal" as opposed to the "bi-covenantal" scheme of classical federal theology.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201048-53) Conservative contemporary Reformed theologians, such as [John Murray](/wiki/John_Murray_(theologian) "John Murray (theologian)"), have also rejected the idea of covenants based on law rather than grace. [Michael Horton](/wiki/Michael_Horton_(theologian) "Michael Horton (theologian)"), however, has defended the covenant of works as combining principles of law and love.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorton2011a420–421-54)

### God

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: God")]

See also: [God in Christianity](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") and [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg/250px-Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg)

The [Shield of the Trinity](/wiki/Shield_of_the_Trinity "Shield of the Trinity") diagrams the classic doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity").

For the most part, the Reformed tradition did not modify the medieval consensus on the [doctrine of God](/wiki/Doctrine_of_God "Doctrine of God").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201054-55) God's character is described primarily using three adjectives: eternal, infinite, and unchangeable.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201055-56) Reformed theologians such as [Shirley Guthrie](/wiki/Shirley_Guthrie "Shirley Guthrie") have proposed that rather than conceiving of God in terms of his attributes and freedom to do as he pleases, the doctrine of God is to be based on God's work in history and his freedom to live with and empower people.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201057–58-57)

Reformed theologians have also traditionally followed the medieval tradition going back to before the early church councils of [Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") and [Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") on the doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"). God is affirmed to be one God in three persons: [Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"), and [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"). The Son (Christ) is held to be eternally begotten by the Father and the [Holy Spirit eternally proceeding](/wiki/Procession_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Procession of the Holy Spirit") from the Father and Son.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201061–62-58) However, contemporary theologians have been critical of aspects of Western views here as well. Drawing on the [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") tradition, these Reformed theologians have proposed a "[social trinitarianism](/wiki/Social_trinitarianism "Social trinitarianism")" where the persons of the Trinity only exist in their life together as persons-in-relationship.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201061–62-58) Contemporary Reformed confessions such as the [Barmen Confession](/wiki/Barmen_Confession "Barmen Confession") and Brief Statement of Faith of the Presbyterian Church (USA) have avoided language about the attributes of God and have emphasized his work of reconciliation and empowerment of people.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuthrie200832–33-59) Feminist theologian [Letty Russell](/wiki/Letty_Russell "Letty Russell") used the image of partnership for the persons of the Trinity. According to Russell, thinking this way encourages Christians to interact in terms of fellowship rather than reciprocity.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200129-60) Conservative Reformed theologian Michael Horton, however, has argued that social trinitarianism is untenable because it abandons the essential unity of God in favor of a community of separate beings.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorton2011a298–299-61)

### Christ and atonement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Christ and atonement")]

See also: [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ"), [Hypostatic union](/wiki/Hypostatic_union "Hypostatic union"), [Extra calvinisticum](/wiki/Extra_calvinisticum "Extra calvinisticum"), [Substitutionary atonement](/wiki/Substitutionary_atonement "Substitutionary atonement"), and [Threefold office](/wiki/Threefold_office "Threefold office")

Reformed theologians affirm the historic Christian belief that [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") is eternally [one person with a divine and a human nature](/wiki/Hypostatic_union "Hypostatic union"). Reformed Christians have especially emphasized that Christ truly [became human](/wiki/Incarnation_of_Christ "Incarnation of Christ") so that people could be saved.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200182-62) Christ's human nature has been a point of contention between Reformed and Lutheran [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology"). In accord with the belief that finite humans cannot comprehend infinite divinity, Reformed theologians hold that Christ's human body cannot be in multiple locations at the same time. Because [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") believe that Christ is bodily [present in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence "Real presence"), they hold that Christ is bodily present in many locations simultaneously. For Reformed Christians, such a belief denies that Christ actually became human.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201065–66-63) Some contemporary Reformed theologians have moved away from the traditional language of one person in two natures, viewing it as unintelligible to contemporary people. Instead, theologians tend to emphasize Jesus's context and particularity as a first-century Jew.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStroup1996142-64)

John Calvin and many Reformed theologians who followed him describe Christ's work of redemption in terms of [three offices](/wiki/Threefold_office "Threefold office"): [prophet](/wiki/Prophet "Prophet"), [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest"), and [king](/wiki/Christ_the_King "Christ the King"). Christ is said to be a prophet in that he teaches perfect doctrine, a priest in that [he intercedes to the Father](/wiki/Intercession_of_Christ "Intercession of Christ") on believers' behalf and offered himself as a sacrifice for sin, and a king in that he rules the church and fights on believers' behalf. The threefold office links the work of Christ to God's work in [ancient Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(united_monarchy) "Kingdom of Israel (united monarchy)").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200194-65) Many, but not all, Reformed theologians continue to make use of the threefold office as a framework because of its emphasis on the connection of Christ's work to Israel. They have, however, often reinterpreted the meaning of each of the offices.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStroup1996156–157-66) For example, Karl Barth interpreted Christ's prophetic office in terms of political engagement on behalf of the poor.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStroup1996164-67)

Christians believe [Jesus' death](/wiki/Jesus%27_death "Jesus' death") and [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") make it possible for believers to receive forgiveness for sin and reconciliation with God through the [atonement](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity"). Reformed Protestants generally subscribe to a particular view of the atonement called [penal substitutionary atonement](/wiki/Penal_substitution "Penal substitution"), which explains Christ's death as a sacrificial payment for sin. Christ is believed to have died in place of the believer, who is accounted righteous as a result of this sacrificial payment.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200193-68)

### Sin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sin")]

See also: [Christian views on sin](/wiki/Christian_views_on_sin "Christian views on sin"), [Total depravity](/wiki/Total_depravity "Total depravity"), and [Original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin")

In Christian theology, people are created good and in the [image of God](/wiki/Image_of_God "Image of God") but have become corrupted by [sin](/wiki/Sin "Sin"), which causes them to be imperfect and overly self-interested.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200166-69) Reformed Christians, following the tradition of [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo"), believe that this corruption of human nature was brought on by Adam and Eve's first sin, a doctrine called [original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin").

Although earlier Christian authors taught the elements of physical death, moral weakness, and a sin propensity within original sin, Augustine was the first Christian to add the concept of inherited guilt (*reatus*) from Adam whereby every infant is born eternally damned and humans lack any residual ability to respond to God.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Reformed theologians emphasize that this sinfulness affects all of a person's nature, including their will. This view, that sin so dominates people that they are unable to avoid sin, has been called [total depravity](/wiki/Total_depravity "Total depravity").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim200171–72-71) As a consequence, every one of their descendants inherited a stain of corruption and depravity. This condition, innate to all humans, is known in Christian theology as *original sin*.

Calvin thought original sin was "a hereditary corruption and depravity of our nature, extending to all the parts of the soul." Calvin asserted people were so warped by original sin that "everything which our mind conceives, meditates, plans, and resolves, is always evil." The depraved condition of every human being is not the result of sins people commit during their lives. Instead, before we are born, while we are in our mother's womb, "we are in God's sight defiled and polluted." Calvin thought people were justly condemned to hell because their corrupted state is "naturally hateful to God."[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In colloquial English, the term "total depravity" can be easily misunderstood to mean that people are absent of any goodness or unable to do any good. However the Reformed teaching is actually that while people continue to bear God's image and may do things that appear outwardly good, their sinful intentions affect all of their nature and actions so that they are not pleasing to God.[[72]](#cite_note-muller_2012-73): 51

### Salvation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Salvation")]

See also: [Sola fide](/wiki/Sola_fide "Sola fide"), [Justification](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)"), and [Sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Rembrandt_Harmensz_van_Rijn_-_Return_of_the_Prodigal_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/330px-Rembrandt_Harmensz_van_Rijn_-_Return_of_the_Prodigal_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rembrandt_Harmensz_van_Rijn_-_Return_of_the_Prodigal_Son_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

The [Parable of the Prodigal Son](/wiki/Parable_of_the_Prodigal_Son "Parable of the Prodigal Son"), depicted in a portrait by [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt"), illustrates forgiveness.

Reformed theologians, along with other Protestants, believe salvation from punishment for sin is to be given to all those who have [faith](/wiki/Faith_in_Christianity "Faith in Christianity") in Christ.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201077–78-74) Faith is not purely intellectual, but involves trust in God's promise to save.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001114-75) Protestants do not hold there to be any other requirement for salvation, but that [faith alone](/wiki/Faith_alone "Faith alone") is sufficient.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201077–78-74) However, this faith in the Lord Jesus is understood as one that effects obedience. In a commentary on Ezekiel 18, Calvin stated: "faith cannot justify when it is without works, because it is dead, and a mere fiction ... Thus faith can be no more separated from works than the sun from his heat."[[75]](#cite_note-76)

[Justification](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)") is the part of salvation where God pardons the sin of those who believe in Christ. It is historically held by Protestants to be the most important article of Christian faith, though more recently it is sometimes given less importance out of [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenical "Ecumenical") concerns.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201080-77) People are not on their own able to fully [repent](/wiki/Repentance_in_Christianity "Repentance in Christianity") of their sin or prepare themselves to repent because of their sinfulness. Therefore, justification is held to arise solely from God's free and gracious act.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001113-78)

[Sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity") is the part of salvation in which God makes believers holy, by enabling them to exercise greater love for God and for other people.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201084-79) The [good works](/wiki/Good_works "Good works") accomplished by believers as they are sanctified are considered to be the necessary outworking of the believer's salvation, though they do not cause the believer to be saved.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001114-75) Sanctification, like justification, is by faith, because doing good works is simply living as the child of God one has become.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201085-80)

### Predestination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Predestination")]

Main article: [Predestination in Calvinism](/wiki/Predestination_in_Calvinism "Predestination in Calvinism")

Stemming from the [theology of John Calvin](/wiki/Theology_of_John_Calvin "Theology of John Calvin"), Reformed theologians teach that sin so affects human nature that they are unable even to exercise faith in Christ by their own will. While people are said to retain free will, in that they willfully sin, they are unable not to sin because of the corruption of their nature due to original sin. Reformed Christians believe that God [predestined](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") some people to be saved and others were predestined to eternal damnation.[[80]](#cite_note-81) This [choice by God to save some is held to be unconditional](/wiki/Unconditional_election "Unconditional election") and not based on any characteristic or action on the part of the person chosen. The Calvinist view is opposed to the [Arminian](/wiki/Arminian "Arminian") view that God's [choice of whom to save is conditional](/wiki/Conditional_election "Conditional election") or based on his foreknowledge of who would respond positively to God.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2010100–101-82)

Karl Barth reinterpreted the doctrine of predestination to apply only to Christ. Individual people are only said to be elected through their being in Christ.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001229–230-83) Reformed theologians who followed Barth, including [Jürgen Moltmann](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Moltmann "Jürgen Moltmann"), David Migliore, and [Shirley Guthrie](/wiki/Shirley_Guthrie "Shirley Guthrie"), have argued that the traditional Reformed concept of predestination is speculative and have proposed alternative models. These theologians claim that a properly trinitarian doctrine emphasizes God's freedom to love all people, rather than choosing some for salvation and others for damnation. God's justice towards and condemnation of sinful people is spoken of by these theologians as out of his love for them and a desire to reconcile them to himself.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuthrie200847–49-84)

#### Five Points of Calvinism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Five Points of Calvinism")]

Main article: [Five Points of Calvinism](/wiki/Five_Points_of_Calvinism "Five Points of Calvinism")
