Much attention surrounding Calvinism focuses on the "Five Points of Calvinism" (also called the *doctrines of grace*).[[84]](#cite_note-85) The five points have been summarized under the [acrostic](/wiki/Acrostic "Acrostic") TULIP.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Another list was proposed by author Lloyd Immanuel Acree as S-I-M-P-L-E, listed S, Segregated Atonement; I, Involuntary Reconciliation; M, Mystery Relationship; P, Preemptive Reprobation; L, Lethargic Election; and E, Effectual Disdain: offered to clear up the negative outcomes represented in but unaddressed by the use of TULIP ( <https://x.com/i/status/2029216047920746902> ) The five points are popularly said to summarize the [Canons of Dort](/wiki/Canons_of_Dort "Canons of Dort"); however, there is no historical relationship between them, and some scholars argue that their language distorts the meaning of the Canons, Calvin's theology, and the theology of 17th-century Calvinistic orthodoxy, particularly in the language of total depravity and limited atonement.[[72]](#cite_note-muller_2012-73) The five points were more recently popularized in the 1963 booklet *The Five Points of Calvinism Defined, Defended, Documented* by David N. Steele and Curtis C. Thomas. The origins of the five points and the acrostic are uncertain, but they appear to be outlined in the [Counter Remonstrance of 1611](/wiki/Counter_Remonstrance_of_1611 "Counter Remonstrance of 1611"), a lesser-known Reformed reply to the Arminians, which was written prior to the Canons of Dort.[[86]](#cite_note-87) The acrostic was used by [Cleland Boyd McAfee](/wiki/Cleland_Boyd_McAfee "Cleland Boyd McAfee") as early as circa 1905.[[87]](#cite_note-88) An early printed appearance of the acrostic can be found in Loraine Boettner's 1932 book, *The Reformed Doctrine of Predestination*.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

### Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Church")]

See also: [Protestant ecclesiology](/wiki/Protestant_ecclesiology "Protestant ecclesiology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/John_Calvin_on_his_deathbed%2C_with_members_of_the_Church_in_a_Wellcome_V0006910.jpg/330px-John_Calvin_on_his_deathbed%2C_with_members_of_the_Church_in_a_Wellcome_V0006910.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Calvin_on_his_deathbed,_with_members_of_the_Church_in_a_Wellcome_V0006910.jpg)

John Calvin depicted on his deathbed with church members in *The last moments of Calvin*, a late 19th century portrait by [Lluís Domènech i Montaner](/wiki/Llu%C3%ADs_Dom%C3%A8nech_i_Montaner "Lluís Domènech i Montaner")

Reformed Christians see the [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") as the community with which God has made the covenant of grace, a promise of eternal life and relationship with God. This covenant extends to those under the "old covenant" whom God chose, beginning with [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham "Abraham") and [Sarah](/wiki/Sarah "Sarah").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001125-90) The church is conceived of as both [invisible](/wiki/Invisible_church "Invisible church") and [visible](/wiki/Visible_church "Visible church"). The invisible church is the body of all believers, known only to God. The visible church is the institutional body which contains both members of the invisible church as well as those who appear to have faith in Christ, but are not truly part of God's elect.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001126-91)

In order to identify the visible church, Reformed theologians have spoken of certain [marks of the Church](/wiki/Marks_of_the_Church_(Protestantism) "Marks of the Church (Protestantism)"). For some, the only mark is the pure preaching of the gospel of Christ. Others, including John Calvin, also include the right administration of the [sacraments](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament"). Others, such as those following the [Scots Confession](/wiki/Scots_Confession "Scots Confession"), include a third mark of rightly administered [church discipline](/wiki/Church_discipline "Church discipline"), or exercise of censure against unrepentant sinners. These marks allowed the Reformed to identify the church based on its [conformity](/wiki/Conformity "Conformity") to the Bible rather than the [magisterium](/wiki/Magisterium "Magisterium") or church tradition.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKim2001126-91)

### Worship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Worship")]

Main article: [Reformed worship](/wiki/Reformed_worship "Reformed worship")

#### Regulative principle of worship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Regulative principle of worship")]

Main article: [Regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Directory_for_Public_Worship.jpg/250px-Directory_for_Public_Worship.jpg)](/wiki/File:Directory_for_Public_Worship.jpg)

The [Directory for Public Worship](/wiki/Directory_for_Public_Worship "Directory for Public Worship") described what should (and should not) occur in worship.

The regulative principle of worship is a teaching shared by some Calvinists and [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptists "Anabaptists") on how the Bible orders public worship. The substance of the doctrine regarding worship is that God institutes in the Scriptures everything he requires for worship in the Church and that everything else is prohibited. As the regulative principle is reflected in Calvin's own thought, it is driven by his evident antipathy toward the Roman Catholic Church and its worship practices, and it associates musical instruments with [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon"), which he considered violations of the [Ten Commandments](/wiki/Ten_Commandments "Ten Commandments")' prohibition of graven images.[[91]](#cite_note-Barber-92)

On this basis, many early Calvinists also eschewed musical instruments and advocated [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") [exclusive psalmody](/wiki/Exclusive_psalmody "Exclusive psalmody") in worship,[[92]](#cite_note-93) though Calvin himself allowed other scriptural songs as well as psalms,[[91]](#cite_note-Barber-92) and this practice typified [Presbyterian worship](/wiki/Presbyterian_worship "Presbyterian worship") and the worship of other Reformed churches for some time.[[93]](#cite_note-94) The original Lord's Day service designed by John Calvin was a highly liturgical service with the Creed, Alms, Confession and Absolution, the Lord's supper, Doxologies, prayers, Psalms being sung, the Lords prayer being sung, and Benedictions.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

Since the 19th century, however, some of the Reformed churches have modified their understanding of the regulative principle and make use of musical instruments, believing that Calvin and his early followers went beyond the biblical requirements[[91]](#cite_note-Barber-92) and that such things are circumstances of worship requiring biblically rooted wisdom, rather than an explicit command. Despite the protestations of those who hold to a strict view of the regulative principle, today [hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") and musical instruments are in common use, as are [contemporary worship music](/wiki/Contemporary_worship_music "Contemporary worship music") styles with elements such as [worship bands](/wiki/Worship_band "Worship band").[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### Sacraments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sacraments")]

See also: [Reformed teaching on sacraments](/wiki/Sacrament#Reformed_(Continental_Reformed,_Congregationalist,_and_Presbyterian) "Sacrament"), [Reformed baptismal theology](/wiki/Reformed_baptismal_theology "Reformed baptismal theology"), and [Lord's Supper in Reformed theology](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology")

The [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith") limits the sacraments to baptism and the Lord's Supper. Sacraments are denoted "signs and seals of the covenant of grace."[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWCF1646[[s:Westminster_Confession_of_Faith#CHAPTER_XVIII._Of_the_Assurance_of_Grace_and_Salvation.|XXVII.I]]-97) Westminster speaks of "a sacramental relation, or a sacramental union, between the sign and the thing signified; whence it comes to pass that the names and effects of the one are attributed to the other."[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWCF1646[[s:Westminster_Confession_of_Faith#CHAPTER_XXVII._Of_the_Sacraments.|XXVII.II]]-98) Baptism is for infant children of believers as well as believers, as it is for all the Reformed except [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") and some [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists"). Baptism admits the baptized into the [visible church](/wiki/Visible_church "Visible church"), and in it all the benefits of Christ are offered to the baptized.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWCF1646[[s:Westminster_Confession_of_Faith#CHAPTER_XXVII._Of_the_Sacraments.|XXVII.II]]-98) On the Lord's supper, the Westminster Confession takes a position between Lutheran sacramental union and Zwinglian memorialism: "the Lord's supper really and indeed, yet not carnally and corporally, but spiritually, receive and feed upon Christ crucified, and all benefits of his death: the body and blood of Christ being then not corporally or carnally in, with, or under the bread and wine; yet, as really, but spiritually, present to the faith of believers in that ordinance as the elements themselves are to their outward senses."[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWCF1646[[s:Westminster_Confession_of_Faith#CHAPTER_XVIII._Of_the_Assurance_of_Grace_and_Salvation.|XXVII.I]]-97)

The [1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith](/wiki/1689_London_Baptist_Confession_of_Faith "1689 London Baptist Confession of Faith") does not use the term sacrament, but describes baptism and the Lord's supper as ordinances, as do most Baptists, Calvinist or otherwise. Baptism is only for those who "actually profess repentance towards God", and not for the children of believers.[[98]](#cite_note-99) Baptists also insist on immersion or dipping, in contradistinction to other Reformed Christians.[[99]](#cite_note-100) The Baptist Confession describes the Lord's supper as "the body and blood of Christ being then not corporally or carnally, but spiritually present to the faith of believers in that ordinance", similarly to the Westminster Confession.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWCF1646[[s:Westminster_Confession_of_Faith#CHAPTER_XXIX._Of_the_Lord's_Supper.|XXIX.VII]]-101) There is significant latitude in Baptist congregations regarding the Lord's supper, and many hold the Zwinglian view.

### Logical order of God's decree

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Logical order of God's decree")]

Main article: [Logical order of God's decree](/wiki/Logical_order_of_God%27s_decree "Logical order of God's decree")

There are two schools of thought regarding the logical order of God's decree to ordain the fall of man: [supralapsarianism](/wiki/Supralapsarianism "Supralapsarianism") (from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *supra*, "above", here meaning "before" + *lapsus*, "fall") and [infralapsarianism](/wiki/Infralapsarianism "Infralapsarianism") (from the Latin: *infra*, "beneath", here meaning "after" + **lapsus**, "fall"). The former view, sometimes called "high Calvinism", argues that the Fall occurred partly to facilitate God's purpose to choose some individuals for salvation and some for damnation. Infralapsarianism, sometimes called "low Calvinism", is the position that, while the Fall was indeed planned, it was not planned with reference to who would be saved.

Supralapsarianism is based on the belief that God chose which individuals to save logically prior to the decision to allow the race to fall and that the Fall serves as the means of realization of that prior decision to send some individuals to hell and others to heaven (that is, it provides the grounds of condemnation in the reprobate and the need for salvation in the elect). In contrast, infralapsarians hold that God planned the race to fall logically prior to the decision to save or damn any individuals because, it is argued, in order to be "saved", one must first need to be saved from something and therefore the decree of the Fall must precede predestination to salvation or damnation.

These two views vied with each other at the Synod of Dort, an international body representing Calvinist Christian churches from around Europe, and the judgments that came out of that council sided with infralapsarianism (Canons of Dort, First Point of Doctrine, Article 7). The Westminster Confession of Faith also teaches (in Hodge's words "clearly impl[ies]") the infralapsarian[[101]](#cite_note-102) view, but is sensitive to those holding to supralapsarianism.[[102]](#cite_note-103) The Lapsarian controversy has a few vocal proponents on each side today, but overall it does not receive much attention among modern Calvinists.

## Branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Branches")]

The Reformed tradition is historically represented by the [Continental](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), [Reformed Anglican](/wiki/Evangelical_Anglican "Evangelical Anglican"), [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist_church "Congregationalist church"), Calvinistic Methodist and [Reformed Baptist](/wiki/Reformed_Baptist "Reformed Baptist") denominational families.

Reformed churches practice several forms of [church government](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity"), primarily [presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity") and [congregational](/wiki/Congregational_polity "Congregational polity"), but some adhere to [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") polity. The largest interdenominational association is the [World Communion of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Communion_of_Reformed_Churches "World Communion of Reformed Churches") with more than 100 million members in 211 member denominations around the world.[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) Smaller, conservative Reformed associations include the [World Reformed Fellowship](/wiki/World_Reformed_Fellowship "World Reformed Fellowship") and the [International Conference of Reformed Churches](/wiki/International_Conference_of_Reformed_Churches "International Conference of Reformed Churches").

### Continental

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Continental")]

Main article: [Continental Reformed Protestantism](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism")

"Continental" Reformed churches originate in [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), a term used by English speakers to distinguish them from traditions from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). Many uphold the [Helvetic Confessions](/wiki/Helvetic_Confessions "Helvetic Confessions") and [Heidelberg Catechism](/wiki/Heidelberg_Catechism "Heidelberg Catechism"), which were adopted in Zurich and Heidelberg, respectively.[[105]](#cite_note-Schaff1898-106) In the United States, immigrants belonging to the continental Reformed churches joined the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church") there, as well as the Anglican Church.[[106]](#cite_note-Conkin1995-107)

### Presbyterian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Presbyterian")]

Main article: [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism")

[Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") churches are named for their order of government by assemblies of [elders](/wiki/Elder_(Christianity) "Elder (Christianity)"), or *presbyters*. They are especially influenced by [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox"), who brought Reformed theology and polity to the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") after spending time on the continent in [Calvin's](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"). Presbyterians historically uphold the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith").

### Congregational

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Congregational")]

Main article: [Congregationalism](/wiki/Congregationalism "Congregationalism")

Congregationalism originates in [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism"), a sixteenth-century movement to reform the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Unlike the Presbyterians, Congregationalists consider the local church to be rightfully [self-ruled](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy") by their own officers, not higher ecclesiastical courts. The [Savoy Declaration](/wiki/Savoy_Declaration "Savoy Declaration"), a revision of Westminster, is the primary [confession](/wiki/Confession_of_faith "Confession of faith") of historic Congregationalism.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Evangelical Congregationalists are internationally represented by the [World Evangelical Congregational Fellowship](/wiki/World_Evangelical_Congregational_Fellowship "World Evangelical Congregational Fellowship"). Christian denominations in the Congregationalist tradition include the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ"), the [National Association of Congregational Christian Churches](/wiki/National_Association_of_Congregational_Christian_Churches "National Association of Congregational Christian Churches") and the [Conservative Congregational Christian Conference](/wiki/Conservative_Congregational_Christian_Conference "Conservative Congregational Christian Conference") in the United States, [Evangelical Congregational Church in Argentina](/wiki/Evangelical_Congregational_Church_in_Argentina "Evangelical Congregational Church in Argentina") and [Evangelical Fellowship of Congregational Churches](/wiki/Evangelical_Fellowship_of_Congregational_Churches "Evangelical Fellowship of Congregational Churches") in the United Kingdom, among others.

### Anglican

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Anglican")]

Main article: [Reformed theology in Anglicanism](/wiki/Reformed_theology_in_Anglicanism "Reformed theology in Anglicanism")

Though Anglicanism today is often described its own branch of Protestantism, historic Anglicanism is a part of the wider Reformed tradition. The foundational documents of the Anglican church "express a theology in keeping with the Reformed theology of the Swiss and South German Reformation."[[108]](#cite_note-Jensen2015-109) The Most Rev. Peter Robinson, [presiding bishop](/wiki/Presiding_bishop "Presiding bishop") of the [United Episcopal Church of North America](/wiki/United_Episcopal_Church_of_North_America "United Episcopal Church of North America"), writes:[[109]](#cite_note-Robinson2012-110)

> Cranmer's personal journey of faith left its mark on the Church of England in the form of a Liturgy that remains to this day more closely allied to Lutheran practice, but that liturgy is couple to a doctrinal stance that is broadly, but decidedly Reformed. ... The 42 Articles of 1552 and the [39 Articles](/wiki/39_Articles "39 Articles") of 1563, both commit the Church of England to the fundamentals of the Reformed Faith. Both sets of Articles affirm the centrality of Scripture, and take a [monergist](/wiki/Monergism "Monergism") position on Justification. Both sets of Articles affirm that the Church of England accepts the doctrine of predestination and election as a 'comfort to the faithful' but warn against over much speculation concerning that doctrine. Indeed a casual reading of the Wurttemburg Confession of 1551,[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005751-111) the Second Helvetic Confession, the Scots Confession of 1560, and the XXXIX Articles of Religion reveal them to be cut from the same bolt of cloth.[[109]](#cite_note-Robinson2012-110)

### Reformed Methodist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Reformed Methodist")]

Reformed Methodists, also known as Calvinistic Methodists, form a minority of the [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") tradition. The majority of Methodism falls outside the Reformed faith, being [Wesleyan Methodism](/wiki/Wesleyan_theology "Wesleyan theology"), which subscribes to Wesleyan-Arminian theology. Calvinistic Methodists adhere to Reformed theology codified in the "Confession of Faith of the Calvinistic Methodists" (1823).[[111]](#cite_note-112) In the United Kingdom, the [Calvinistic Methodist Church](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Wales "Presbyterian Church of Wales") is also known as the Presbyterian Church of Wales. Calvinistic Methodists are characterized by their emphasis on Methodist worship distinctives—preaching, hymn singing, lovefeasts, revival services, and camp meetings, as well as the Methodist doctrines of the [New Birth](/wiki/Born_again "Born again") and [growth in grace](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity"). Reformed Methodist divines include [George Whitefield](/wiki/George_Whitefield "George Whitefield") and [Howell Harris](/wiki/Howell_Harris "Howell Harris").[[112]](#cite_note-113)

### Reformed Baptist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Reformed Baptist")]

Main article: [Reformed Baptists](/wiki/Reformed_Baptists "Reformed Baptists")

Reformed Baptists or Calvinistic [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"),[[113]](#cite_note-114) unlike other Reformed groups, exclusively practice [believer's baptism](/wiki/Believer%27s_baptism "Believer's baptism"). They observe a more congregational polity, taken from the Congregationalists. Their primary confession is the [Second London Confession of Faith](/wiki/1689_Baptist_Confession_of_Faith "1689 Baptist Confession of Faith") of 1689, a revision of the [Savoy Declaration](/wiki/Savoy_Declaration "Savoy Declaration") from the Congregationalists, and the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"), from the Presbyterians, but other Baptist confessions like the [First London Confession](/wiki/1644_Baptist_Confession_of_Faith "1644 Baptist Confession of Faith") are also used.[[114]](#cite_note-Hicks2017-115) Not all Baptists are Particular Baptists, and, in fact, the Baptist tradition didn't start Particular Baptist, but [General Baptist](/wiki/General_Baptist "General Baptist").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Many Reformed Baptists accept Reformed theology, especially [soteriology](/wiki/Five_Points_of_Calvinism "Five Points of Calvinism"), and a [covenantal theology](/wiki/Covenantal_theology "Covenantal theology"), named the [Baptist covenant theology](/wiki/Baptist_covenant_theology "Baptist covenant theology").[[115]](#cite_note-116)

## Variants in Reformed theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Variants in Reformed theology")]

### Amyraldism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Amyraldism")]

Main article: [Amyraldism](/wiki/Amyraldism "Amyraldism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Mo%C3%AFse_Amyraut.jpg/250px-Mo%C3%AFse_Amyraut.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mo%C3%AFse_Amyraut.jpg)

[Moses Amyraut](/wiki/Moses_Amyraut "Moses Amyraut") formulated [Amyraldism](/wiki/Amyraldism "Amyraldism"), a modified Calvinist theology regarding the nature of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus")' atonement.[[116]](#cite_note-117)[[117]](#cite_note-118)

Amyraldism (or sometimes Amyraldianism, also known as the School of Saumur, hypothetical universalism,[[118]](#cite_note-119) post redemptionism,[[119]](#cite_note-120) moderate Calvinism,[[120]](#cite_note-121) or four-point Calvinism) is the belief that [God](/wiki/God "God"), prior to his decree of election, decreed [Christ's atonement](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity") for all alike if they believe, but seeing that none would believe on their own, he then [elected](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") those whom he will bring to [faith in Christ](/wiki/Faith_in_Christianity "Faith in Christianity"), thereby preserving the Calvinist doctrine of [unconditional election](/wiki/Unconditional_election "Unconditional election"). The efficacy of the atonement remains limited to those who believe.

Named after its formulator [Moses Amyraut](/wiki/Moses_Amyraut "Moses Amyraut"), this doctrine is still viewed as a variety of Calvinism in that it maintains the particularity of sovereign grace in the application of the atonement. However, detractors like [B. B. Warfield](/wiki/B._B._Warfield "B. B. Warfield") have termed it "an inconsistent and therefore unstable form of Calvinism."[[121]](#cite_note-122)

### Hyper-Calvinism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Hyper-Calvinism")]

Main article: [Hyper-Calvinism](/wiki/Hyper-Calvinism "Hyper-Calvinism")

Hyper-Calvinism is the belief that emphasizes God's sovereignty in election and salvation to such an extent that it rejects the responsibility of all people to "[repent](/wiki/Repentance_in_Christianity "Repentance in Christianity") and believe" the gospel. This belief system became prominent among some of the early English [Particular Baptists](/wiki/Strict_Baptist "Strict Baptist") in the 18th century. Historically, it has been associated with theologians such as [John Gill](/wiki/John_Gill_(theologian) "John Gill (theologian)") and [Joseph Hussey](/wiki/Joseph_Hussey "Joseph Hussey") who contributed to the development of its distinct views.[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[123]](#cite_note-:0-124) This variant of Reformed Theology was opposed by ministers such as [Andrew Fuller](/wiki/Andrew_Fuller "Andrew Fuller") and missionaries such as [William Carey](/wiki/William_Carey_(missionary) "William Carey (missionary)") who argued against the Hyper-Calvinistic mindset that "if God wants to save the heathen, He will do it without your help or mine."[[124]](#cite_note-125)[[125]](#cite_note-126)

The [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith") says that the gospel is to be freely offered to sinners, and the [Larger Catechism](/wiki/Westminster_Larger_Catechism "Westminster Larger Catechism") makes clear that the gospel is offered to the non-elect.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWCF1646[[s:Westminster_Confession_of_Faith#CHAPTER_VII_of_God's_Covenant_with_Man.|VII.III]]-127)[[127]](#cite_note-128)

The term is also used as a pejorative and occasionally appears in both [theological](/wiki/Theological "Theological") and secular controversial contexts. It usually connotes a negative opinion about some variety of [theological determinism](/wiki/Theological_determinism "Theological determinism"), [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination"), or a version of Evangelical Christianity or Calvinism that is deemed by the critic to be unenlightened, harsh, or extreme.

### Neo-Calvinism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Neo-Calvinism")]

Main article: [Neo-Calvinism](/wiki/Neo-Calvinism "Neo-Calvinism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Abraham_Kuyper_1905_%281%29.jpg/250px-Abraham_Kuyper_1905_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Kuyper_1905_(1).jpg)

Dutch prime minister [Abraham Kuyper](/wiki/Abraham_Kuyper "Abraham Kuyper") initiated [Neo-Calvinism](/wiki/Neo-Calvinism "Neo-Calvinism").

Beginning in the 1880s, Neo-Calvinism, a form of [Dutch Calvinism](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church"), is the movement initiated by the theologian and later Dutch prime minister [Abraham Kuyper](/wiki/Abraham_Kuyper "Abraham Kuyper"). [James Bratt](/wiki/James_Bratt "James Bratt") has identified a number of different types of Dutch Calvinism: The Seceders—split into the Reformed Church "West" and the Confessionalists; and the Neo-Calvinists—the Positives and the Antithetical Calvinists. The Seceders were largely [infralapsarian](/wiki/Infralapsarian "Infralapsarian") and the Neo-Calvinists usually [supralapsarian](/wiki/Supralapsarian "Supralapsarian").[[128]](#cite_note-129)

Kuyper wanted to awaken the church from what he viewed as its pietistic slumber. He declared:

> No single piece of our mental world is to be sealed off from the rest and there is not a square inch in the whole domain of human existence over which Christ, who is sovereign over all, does not cry: 'Mine!'[[129]](#cite_note-130)

This refrain has become something of a rallying call for Neo-Calvinists.

### Christian Reconstructionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Christian Reconstructionism")]

Main article: [Christian Reconstructionism](/wiki/Christian_Reconstructionism "Christian Reconstructionism")

Christian Reconstructionism is a [fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism")[[130]](#cite_note-131) Calvinist [theonomic](/wiki/Theonomic "Theonomic") movement that has remained rather obscure.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Founded by [R. J. Rushdoony](/wiki/R._J._Rushdoony "R. J. Rushdoony"), the movement has had an important influence on the [Christian Right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") in the United States.[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134) The movement peaked in the 1990s.[[134]](#cite_note-135) However, it lives on in small denominations such as the [Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States "Reformed Presbyterian Church in the United States") and as a minority position in other denominations. Christian Reconstructionists are usually [postmillennialists](/wiki/Postmillennialist "Postmillennialist") and followers of the [presuppositional apologetics](/wiki/Presuppositional_apologetics "Presuppositional apologetics") of [Cornelius Van Til](/wiki/Cornelius_Van_Til "Cornelius Van Til"). They tend to support a decentralized political order resulting in [laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") capitalism.[[135]](#cite_note-136)

### New Calvinism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: New Calvinism")]

Main article: [New Calvinism](/wiki/New_Calvinism "New Calvinism")

New Calvinism is a growing perspective within conservative Evangelicalism that embraces the fundamentals of 16th century Calvinism while also trying to be relevant in the present day world.[[136]](#cite_note-CTHansen-137) In March 2009, *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine described the New Calvinism as one of the "10 ideas changing the world".[[137]](#cite_note-Time-138) Some of the major figures who have been associated with the New Calvinism are [John Piper](/wiki/John_Piper_(theologian) "John Piper (theologian)"),[[136]](#cite_note-CTHansen-137) [Mark Driscoll](/wiki/Mark_Driscoll_(pastor) "Mark Driscoll (pastor)"), [Al Mohler](/wiki/Al_Mohler "Al Mohler"),[[137]](#cite_note-Time-138) [Mark Dever](/wiki/Mark_Dever "Mark Dever"),[[138]](#cite_note-Burek-139) [C. J. Mahaney](/wiki/C._J._Mahaney "C. J. Mahaney"), and [Tim Keller](/wiki/Timothy_J._Keller "Timothy J. Keller").[[139]](#cite_note-CRN-140) New Calvinists have been criticized for blending Calvinist soteriology with popular Evangelical positions on the [sacraments](/wiki/Sacrament#Reformed_(Continental_Reformed,_Congregationalist,_and_Presbyterian) "Sacrament") and [continuationism](/wiki/Continuationism "Continuationism") and for rejecting tenets seen as crucial to the Reformed faith such as [confessionalism](/wiki/Reformed_confessions_of_faith "Reformed confessions of faith") and [covenant theology](/wiki/Covenant_theology "Covenant theology").[[140]](#cite_note-141)

## Social and economic influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Social and economic influences")]

See also: [Protestant work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic")

Calvin expressed himself on [usury](/wiki/Usury "Usury") in a 1545 letter to a friend, Claude de Sachin, in which he criticized the use of certain passages of scripture invoked by people opposed to the charging of interest. He reinterpreted some of these passages, and suggested that others of them had been rendered irrelevant by changed conditions. He also dismissed the argument (based upon the writings of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")) that it is wrong to charge interest for money because money itself is barren. He said that the walls and the roof of a house are barren, too, but it is permissible to charge someone for allowing him to use them. In the same way, money can be made fruitful.[[141]](#cite_note-142)

He qualified his view, however, by saying that money should be lent to people in dire need without hope of interest, while a modest interest rate of 5% should be permitted in relation to other borrowers.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

In *[The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism](/wiki/The_Protestant_Ethic_and_the_Spirit_of_Capitalism "The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism")*, [Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber") wrote that capitalism in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") evolved when the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") (particularly Calvinist) ethic influenced large numbers of people to engage in work in the secular world, developing their own [enterprises](/wiki/Organization "Organization") and engaging in trade and the accumulation of wealth for investment. In other words, the [Protestant work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic") was an important force behind the unplanned and uncoordinated emergence of modern [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism").[[143]](#cite_note-aura.abdn.ac.uk-144)

Expert researchers and authors have referred to the United States as a "Protestant nation" or "founded on Protestant principles,"[[144]](#cite_note-145)[[145]](#cite_note-146) specifically emphasizing its Calvinist heritage.[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148)

## Politics and society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Reformed_Christianity&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Politics and society")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Martyrs_of_Guernsey_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Martyrs_of_Guernsey_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martyrs_of_Guernsey_(cropped).jpg)

The burning of the [Guernsey Martyrs](/wiki/Guernsey_Martyrs "Guernsey Martyrs") during the Marian persecutions in 1556

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Bocskai_and_his_hajd%C3%BA_warriors.jpg/330px-Bocskai_and_his_hajd%C3%BA_warriors.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bocskai_and_his_hajd%C3%BA_warriors.jpg)

[Stephen Bocskai](/wiki/Stephen_Bocskai "Stephen Bocskai"), leader of [Hungarian Calvinists](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_Hungary "Reformed Church in Hungary") in the anti-Habsburg rebellion and first Calvinist [prince of Transylvania](/wiki/Principality_of_Transylvania_(1570%E2%80%931711) "Principality of Transylvania (1570–1711)") (r. 1605–1606)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Rijnwoude_019.jpg/330px-Rijnwoude_019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rijnwoude_019.jpg)

A Reformed church in [Koudekerk aan den Rijn](/wiki/Koudekerk_aan_den_Rijn "Koudekerk aan den Rijn") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") in the 19th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Market_Place_at_Haarlem%2C_Looking_towards_Grote_Kerk_by_Berckheyde.jpg/330px-Market_Place_at_Haarlem%2C_Looking_towards_Grote_Kerk_by_Berckheyde.jpg)](/wiki/File:Market_Place_at_Haarlem,_Looking_towards_Grote_Kerk_by_Berckheyde.jpg)

The [Grote Kerk](/wiki/Grote_Kerk,_Haarlem "Grote Kerk, Haarlem") in [Haarlem](/wiki/Haarlem "Haarlem") in the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), c. 1665

Calvin's concepts of God and man led to ideas which were gradually put into practice after his death, in particular in the fields of politics and society. After their fight for independence from Spain (1579), the Netherlands, under Calvinist leadership, granted asylum to religious minorities, including French [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot"), English [Independents](/wiki/Independent_(religion) "Independent (religion)") ([Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists")), and [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") from Spain and Portugal. The ancestors of the philosopher [Baruch Spinoza](/wiki/Baruch_Spinoza "Baruch Spinoza") were Portuguese Jews. Aware of the trial against [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo "Galileo"), [René Descartes](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Descartes "René Descartes") lived in the Netherlands, out of reach of the [Inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition "Inquisition"), from 1628 to 1649.[[148]](#cite_note-149) [Pierre Bayle](/wiki/Pierre_Bayle "Pierre Bayle"), a Reformed Frenchman, also felt safer in the Netherlands than in his home country. He was the first prominent philosopher who demanded tolerance for atheists. [Hugo Grotius](/wiki/Hugo_Grotius "Hugo Grotius") (1583–1645) was able to publish a rather liberal interpretation of the Bible and his ideas about [natural law](/wiki/Natural_law "Natural law") in the Netherlands.[[149]](#cite_note-150)[[150]](#cite_note-151) Moreover, the Calvinist Dutch authorities allowed the printing of books that could not be published elsewhere, such as Galileo's [*Discorsi*](/wiki/Two_New_Sciences "Two New Sciences") (1638).[[151]](#cite_note-152)

Alongside the liberal development of the Netherlands came the rise of modern [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") in England and North America. In the Middle Ages, state and church had been closely connected. [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther")'s [doctrine of the two kingdoms](/wiki/Doctrine_of_the_two_kingdoms "Doctrine of the two kingdoms") separated state and church in principle.[[152]](#cite_note-153) His doctrine of the [priesthood of all believers](/wiki/Priesthood_of_all_believers "Priesthood of all believers") raised the laity to the same level as the clergy,[[153]](#cite_note-154) although Lutherans were content to allow the state to control the administration of the church.

In [Geneva](/wiki/Calvin%27s_Geneva "Calvin's Geneva") Calvin was more careful than Luther to keep church structures and city authorities apart[[154]](#cite_note-155) and going one step further than Luther he included elected laymen ([church elders](/wiki/Church_elder "Church elder"), [presbyters](/wiki/Presbyters "Presbyters")) in his concept of [church government](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity"). In general the Reformed followed Calvin's lead in insisting that the church's external administration, including the right to [excommunicate](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication"), not be handed over to the state.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVanDrunen2007-156) The Huguenots added [synods](/wiki/Synod "Synod") whose members were also elected by the congregations. The other Reformed churches took over this system of church self-government, which was essentially a representative democracy.[[156]](#cite_note-157) [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists") are organized in a similar way. These denominations and the [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church "Anglican Church") were influenced by Calvin's theology in varying degrees.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)

In another factor in the rise of democracy in the Anglo-American world, Calvin favored a mixture of democracy and aristocracy as the best form of government ([mixed government](/wiki/Mixed_government "Mixed government")). He appreciated the advantages of democracy.[[159]](#cite_note-160) His political thought aimed to safeguard the rights and freedoms of ordinary men and women. In order to minimize the misuse of political power he suggested dividing it among several institutions in a system of checks and balances ([separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Finally, Calvin taught that if worldly rulers rise up against God they should be put down. In this way, he and his followers stood in the vanguard of resistance to political [absolutism](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") and furthered the cause of democracy,[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlmstead196010-161) although Calvin himself was alarmed about his arguments being used for revolutionary movements.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacCulloch2009657-162) The [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists") who founded [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony") (1620) and [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") (1628) were convinced that the democratic form of government was the will of God.[[162]](#cite_note-163) Enjoying self-rule, they practiced separation of powers.[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165) [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), founded by [Roger Williams](/wiki/Roger_Williams "Roger Williams"), [Thomas Hooker](/wiki/Thomas_Hooker "Thomas Hooker"), and [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), respectively, combined democratic government with a limited [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") that did not extend to Catholics (Congregationalism being the established, tax-supported religion in Connecticut).[[165]](#cite_note-166) These colonies became safe havens for persecuted religious minorities, including [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews").[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlmstead196074–76,_99–117-167)[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169)

In [England](/wiki/England "England"), Baptists [Thomas Helwys](/wiki/Thomas_Helwys "Thomas Helwys") (c. 1575–c. 1616), and [John Smyth](/wiki/John_Smyth_(Baptist_minister) "John Smyth (Baptist minister)") (c. 1554–c. 1612) influenced the liberal political thought of the Presbyterian poet and politician [John Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") (1608–1674) and of the philosopher [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke") (1632–1704),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] who in turn had both a strong impact on the political development in their home country ([English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") of 1642–1651, [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688) as well as in North America.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) The ideological basis of the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") was largely provided by the radical [Whigs](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)"), who had been inspired by Milton, Locke, [James Harrington](/wiki/James_Harrington_(author) "James Harrington (author)") (1611–1677), [Algernon Sidney](/wiki/Algernon_Sidney "Algernon Sidney") (1623–1683), and other thinkers. The Whigs' "perceptions of politics attracted widespread support in America because they revived the traditional concerns of a Protestantism that had always verged on [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism")".[[171]](#cite_note-172) The [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") and (American) [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") initiated a tradition of human and civil rights that continued in the French [Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen](/wiki/Declaration_of_the_Rights_of_Man_and_of_the_Citizen "Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen") and the constitutions of numerous countries around the world, e.g. Latin America, Japan, India, Germany, and other European countries. It is also echoed in the [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter") and the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights").[[172]](#cite_note-173)

In the 19th century, churches based on or influenced by Calvin's theology became deeply involved in social reforms, e.g. the [abolition of slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") ([William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce"), [Harriet Beecher Stowe](/wiki/Harriet_Beecher_Stowe "Harriet Beecher Stowe"), [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), and others), [women suffrage](/wiki/Women_suffrage "Women suffrage"), and [prison reforms](/wiki/Prison_reform "Prison reform").[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlmstead1960353–375-174)[[174]](#cite_note-175) Members of these churches formed [co-operatives](/wiki/Co-operatives "Co-operatives") to help the impoverished masses.[[175]](#cite_note-176) The founders of the [Red Cross Movement](/wiki/Red_Cross_Movement "Red Cross Movement"), including [Henry Dunant](/wiki/Henry_Dunant "Henry Dunant"), were Reformed Christians. Their movement also initiated the [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions").[[176]](#cite_note-177)[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179)

Throughout the world, the Reformed churches operate hospitals, homes for handicapped or elderly people, and educational institutions on all levels. For example, American Congregationalists founded [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") (1636), [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") (1701), and about a dozen other colleges.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlmstead196080,_89,_257-180) A particular stream of influence of Calvinism concerns art. Visual art cemented society in the first modern nation state, the Netherlands, and also Neo-Calvinism put much weight on this aspect of life. [Hans Rookmaaker](/wiki/Hans_Rookmaaker "Hans Rookmaaker") is the most prolific example. In literature the non-fiction of [Marilynne Robinson](/wiki/Marilynne_Robinson "Marilynne Robinson")
argues for the modernity of Calvin's thinking, calling him a humanist scholar (p. 174, The Death of Adam).
